intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards respond better to warm, motivational language than neutral clinical language
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Encouraging tone
facade.acceptImplies: Adopt conversational, supportive microcopy; celebrate small wins; use 'you' language
facade.rejectImplies: Stick to neutral, clinical tone; minimize personality; focus on data presentation over encouragement

evidence.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards respond better to warm, motivational language than neutral clinical language
evidence.implication: Adopt conversational, supportive microcopy; celebrate small wins; use 'you' language

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a persistent sidebar navigation paired with card-based content layout for managing multiple health experiments
facade.label: Sidebar + cards
facade.acceptImplies: Prioritize left-column persistent nav; organize experiment data into discrete, scannable cards rather than dense lists or long-form sections
facade.rejectImplies: Explore top tabs, full-width layouts, or list-heavy designs where navigation is horizontal or context-dependent

facade.hypothesis: A health dashboard benefits from approachable, non-threatening visual softness—rounded elements feel more personal and less clinical than sharp geometric forms
facade.label: Soft rounded corners
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers warm, welcoming visual language; design should emphasize curves, smooth transitions, and organic shapes over rigid geometry
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer clean minimalism or technical precision; design could lean toward crisp edges, grids, and angular clarity to convey data rigor

facade.hypothesis: For health dashboards, animated entrance transitions (elements sliding/fading in) feel more welcoming and living than instant appearance, encouraging users to trust the data as dynamic rather than static.
facade.label: Smooth slide in
facade.acceptImplies: User values fluid, continuous motion and perceives it as more trustworthy for tracking living experiments; future probes should explore transition timing and easing.
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers snappy, immediate data visibility over motion; dashboard should prioritize speed and clarity over fluid transitions.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer prominent metric values over narrative summaries when reviewing health experiment results
facade.axisTargeted: Density of visual hierarchy — whether large quantitative anchors or balanced prose dominates the information layer
facade.label: Big numbers, small text
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard should lead with KPI-style numbers (heart rate: 62 bpm, sleep debt: -4h) in large, glanceable format; narrative context recedes to secondary tier
facade.rejectImplies: Dashboard should pair metrics with equal-weight narrative frames (e.g., 'Your resting heart rate dropped 8 bpm this month. Here's why that matters…') to contextualize numbers before impact registers

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

facade.hypothesis: Users building health dashboards prefer calming, organic visual warmth over clinical sterility
facade.axisTargeted: warm vs cool, organic vs digital
facade.label: Warm tones and soft edges
facade.acceptImplies: The interface should use earthy colors, rounded corners, and natural textures to create a sense of safety and approachability around sensitive health data
facade.rejectImplies: The user may lean toward crisp, cool, minimal aesthetics—clean lines and neutral palettes that feel more scientific or data-focused

facade.hypothesis: Users tracking health experiments prefer conversational, low-pressure language over clinical terminology and strict data-entry framing
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly/conversational vs clinical/formal
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: Use informal language like 'how'd it go?' and 'just checking in' for data entry prompts; frame logging as conversation, not compliance
facade.rejectImplies: Users don't mind or prefer structured, professional language; clinical terms and formal data-entry flows work better than chat-like interactions
